result:
# The Peculiar Magic of Liminal Spaces

There's something about airports at 3 AM that feels like existing outside of time itself. I've been thinking a lot lately about these in-between places—spaces that aren't quite here or there, moments that don't fully belong to one state or another. We have a word for this: liminal, from the Latin *limen*, meaning threshold. But the word itself doesn't quite capture the strange cocktail of emotions these spaces evoke in me, this mixture of melancholy and possibility, of loneliness and freedom.

I find myself drawn to thinking about these transitional zones because they seem to contain something essential about the human experience that we don't talk about enough. We live in a culture obsessed with destinations, with arrivals, with the firm ground of having arrived somewhere definitively. But so much of life is actually spent in the in-between, in the waiting room, in the corridor, in the moment after one thing has ended but before the next thing has truly begun.

Consider the airport terminal in those small hours. The shops are closed, their metal shutters pulled down like sleepy eyelids. A janitor pushes a wide mop across acres of linoleum, the squeak of the handle echoing in the vast space. Travelers slump in uncomfortable chairs, some sleeping with their mouths open, others staring at glowing phone screens, their faces underlit and ghostly. Everything is bathed in that particular quality of fluorescent light that seems to exist nowhere else in nature—not quite white, not quite blue, somehow both harsh and insufficient.

You're not home, but you're not yet at your destination. You're nowhere, really. You're in a space that exists only for the purpose of getting somewhere else. And yet, there's something oddly peaceful about it, isn't there? A strange freedom in being untethered, in being between identities—no longer the person you were where you left, not yet the person you'll need to be when you arrive.

I think about this quality of in-betweenness in other contexts too. The hallways of hotels, especially long ones in large hotels, stretching away with their repetitive patterns of doors and carpet and light fixtures. They're nowhere you're meant to linger, yet they possess an eerie atmosphere that feels almost sacred in its emptiness and purpose. You're just passing through, and yet in that passing, there's a moment of suspension.

Or consider dusk—that liminal time of day that belongs neither to day nor night. The French call it *l'heure bleue*, the blue hour, that period of twilight when the sun has set but darkness hasn't fully arrived. Photographers prize it for the quality of light it offers, soft and directional yet diffused. But it's also a time that has featured in folklore and superstition across cultures as a moment when the veil between worlds grows thin, when magic becomes possible, when the ordinary rules are temporarily suspended.

I've always been someone who feels most alive during transitions. Not in the sense of enjoying change—I'm actually quite resistant to change in many ways—but in the sense of feeling most awake to the texture of experience during these threshold moments. The first week in a new apartment, when your belongings are still in boxes and you haven't yet established routines, when you're not quite sure which cabinet contains the coffee mugs. The last day at a job you're leaving, when you're already mentally halfway out the door but still physically present, going through motions that have become ritual but are about to become memory.

There's a particular quality of attention that these moments demand. You can't be on autopilot in a liminal space. The airport at 3 AM won't let you sleepwalk through it. The unfamiliar apartment makes you conscious of every action—where's the light switch? Which way does the tap turn for hot water? You're forced into a kind of presence, an awareness of the immediate reality around you, precisely because the comfort of habit hasn't yet been established or has been stripped away.

I wonder sometimes if this is why travel is so psychologically valuable, beyond the obvious benefits of new experiences and cultural exposure. Travel puts you in a sustained state of liminality. You're between homes, between identities in some sense. The person you are when traveling—even if you're traveling for business, even if you're doing mundane things—is not quite the same person you are in your daily life. You're yourself, but yourself without the full weight of context and habit and routine. Yourself, but lighter somehow. Or maybe just different.

The pandemic created a strange collective liminal experience that I think many of us are still processing. Those early months especially—March through May of 2020—had this eerie quality of suspension. Time seemed to move differently. We were between the world we'd known and... what? We didn't know. We were in the hallway, the corridor, the waiting room, and we had no idea when we'd reach the door at the other end or what would be on the other side of it.

I remember the empty streets during those early lockdowns, how cities that normally teemed with life became almost post-apocalyptic in their quietness. I would go for walks in my neighborhood at dusk—that liminal time—through streets that felt liminal in their emptiness, in a period of time that felt like the entire world was holding its breath, suspended between a before and an after. It was terrible and anxiety-inducing and yet, if I'm honest, there were moments when it was also strangely beautiful. The quality of light. The silence. The sense of shared uncertainty.

Adolescence is perhaps the most profound liminal period in human life. You're no longer a child but not yet an adult. Your body is changing in ways you can't control. Your sense of yourself is in flux. The rules that governed your childhood no longer quite apply, but the rules of adulthood don't quite fit yet either. It's uncomfortable and confusing and often painful, and yet it's also when many people report feeling most intensely alive, when emotions are most vivid, when experience has a kind of heightened quality that can fade as we settle into more fixed adult identities.

I think there's something instructive in how uncomfortable we generally are with liminality. We want to rush through it. We want to get to the destination, to resolve the uncertainty, to arrive. The metaphor of "being in a good place" reveals our bias—we want to be somewhere stable, somewhere we can put down roots, somewhere that feels like solid ground.

But what if we thought about liminality differently? What if instead of seeing it as something to be rushed through, we recognized it as a space with its own particular value? The waiting room isn't just an annoying delay before the appointment; it's a space where you're briefly freed from obligations, where you can sit and think or read or just watch other people. The hallway isn't just the nothing between the somethings; it's a space of pure potential, of possibility, where you haven't yet committed to what comes next.

There's a concept in Japanese aesthetics called *ma*, often translated as "negative space" or "interval," but really referring to something more subtle—the meaningful void, the pause that gives structure and rhythm to experience. In music, it's the silence between notes. In architecture, it's the empty space that gives shape to the structures around it. In conversation, it's the pause that gives weight to words.

Liminal spaces are a kind of *ma* in lived experience. They're the pauses, the intervals, the spaces between. And just as in music or architecture or conversation, these intervals aren't empty or meaningless. They're essential to the rhythm and structure of a life.

I find myself thinking about stairwells, too—especially those institutional stairwells in office buildings or hospitals or universities. They're spaces almost no one chooses to spend time in unless they're actively moving between floors. And yet they have such a particular character. The echo of footsteps on concrete. The smell of concrete and paint. The way light comes in through narrow windows. They're entirely functional, designed only to facilitate movement from one level to another, and yet in their functionality, in their stripped-down purposefulness, they achieve a kind of austere beauty.

Or parking garages at night, nearly empty, with just a few scattered cars under sodium lights that cast everything in amber. Or train stations in small towns in the middle of the day, when hardly anyone is there. Or 24-hour diners at 4 AM, occupied only by insomniacs and shift workers and travelers.

These spaces feel like they exist in a parallel dimension, almost. They're part of the normal world, but they're the backstage areas, the margins, the spaces between the main events. And there's something about them that feels honest in a way that more polished, finished spaces don't. They're not trying to be anything other than what they are. They're not decorated or designed for comfort or ambiance. They just are. They exist for a purpose, and when that purpose isn't actively being fulfilled, they simply wait, patient and indifferent.

I think this is part of what draws me to liminal spaces—their honesty, their lack of pretense. An airport at 3 AM isn't trying to impress you. A hotel hallway isn't trying to make you feel anything in particular. These spaces reveal the infrastructure of life, the scaffolding that usually hides behind more intentional environments.

As I write this, I'm aware that there's something fundamentally contradictory about trying to capture the essence of liminality in words. Writing about something pins it down, makes it fixed, gives it boundaries and definition. But liminality is by nature unfixed, undefined, boundary-less. It's the threshold, not the room on either side. It's the process, not the product.

And yet, I keep trying to articulate what draws me to these spaces and states because I think there's something important here about how we might live. Our culture pushes us always toward resolution, toward arrival, toward achievement and completion. We're supposed to know what we want and go after it. We're supposed to have goals and plans. We're supposed to be building toward something.

But what if some of the richest moments of life are actually in the unbuilt spaces? In the pauses? In the times when we don't quite know who we are or where we're going? What if being lost is sometimes more valuable than being found?

I don't have answers to these questions. I'm not sure there are answers. But I'm increasingly convinced that learning to be comfortable with liminality—with uncertainty, with transition, with the in-between—is one of the most valuable skills we can develop. Because life is mostly in-between, when you think about it. The destinations are just moments. The journey is everything else.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
